source.title: The hippocampal indexing theory and episodic memory: Updating the index

confidence_rationale: Canonical theoretical grounding for ARC-007's architectural characterisation of hippocampus as indexer rather than value-computer. The indexing theory explicitly assigns hippocampus a structural-index role, providing direct architectural justification for ARC-007's no-value-computation constraint. Confidence reflects strong theoretical alignment with moderate empirical grounding (indexing theory is influential but not uniformly accepted as a complete account).
failure_signatures: Indexing theory assigns hippocampus a pure structural-index role: stores sparse indices to cortical patterns, not values or evaluations.
Pattern completion from partial cue reactivates neocortical episode pattern — hippocampus serves retrieval, not evaluation.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The hippocampus functions as an index: it captures sparse indices of neocortical activity patterns generated by behavioral episodes, enabling later pattern completion retrieval without storing the full neocortical pattern. The hippocampus is architecturally and functionally designed to index, not to evaluate.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-007's 'no value computation' constraint has its canonical theoretical grounding in indexing theory. The hippocampus stores indices to trajectory states in z_world — the path nodes, the state transitions, the sequence — not the value of those states. Value evaluation is the business of E3 and the residue field; the hippocampus provides the trajectory index that E3 retrieves and evaluates. This is a clean architectural division: HPC = structural navigator; E3 = evaluator. Indexing theory predicts that hippocampal damage impairs retrieval of previously formed trajectories but not the evaluation of new information (which would be preserved if cortical structures are intact).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Indexing theory is a functional theory, not a full mechanistic account. Its 'no value computation' implication is an architectural inference from the indexing role, not an explicitly tested prediction. Competing accounts (predictive map theory, Gauthier & Tank dedicated reward cells) challenge the strict neutrality of the indexer view. The theory has been updated since Teyler & DiScenna 1986 to accommodate rapid one-shot learning and context dependency, but the core indexing architecture remains the same.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical review paper — updates Teyler & DiScenna (1986) original indexing theory with 20 years of subsequent evidence. Covers hippocampal-neocortical interactions, pattern completion, and episodic memory.

confidence_components.notes: Theoretical review — inherently lower empirical grounding than experimental papers, but high conceptual relevance. Mapping fidelity is high: the no-value-computation implication is direct and explicit. Transfer risk is low because the theory is domain-general.
